---
title: "Curaçao at the World Cup: what the FIFA ranking does not measure"
subtitle: "Diaspora effects on national-team performance for small states"
author:
  - name: Rendell de Kort
    orcid: 0000-0000-0000-0000
    email: rendell@c-economics.com
    affiliations:
      - name: Cornerstone Economics
      - name: Vrije Universiteit Amsterdam
date: today
date-format: "MMMM YYYY"
abstract: |
  Curaçao qualified for the 2026 FIFA World Cup at world rank 82, the
  smallest country by population ever to do so. The qualification looks
  anomalous against the ranking, but the ranking itself is the source of
  the apparent anomaly. FIFA's Elo-style system rewards frequency of
  matches against high-rated opponents, which structurally disadvantages
  small states that have thin domestic competitions and few qualifying
  fixtures per cycle. For small states with large diasporas the gap is
  larger still, because most of the available talent develops in foreign
  league systems that the ranking does not see. This paper formalises the
  diaspora measurement gap, estimates its size for a panel of small CONCACAF
  and UEFA states, and shows that the FIFA ranking systematically under-
  states realised national-team quality where the diaspora-to-resident
  ratio is high. Curaçao is the most extreme case in the panel and the
  result is generalisable: it predicts comparable outliers in Cabo Verde,
  Albania, and Suriname.
keywords:
  - small-state economics
  - diaspora
  - football
  - measurement
  - FIFA ranking
jel-codes:
  - F22
  - Z22
  - O15
ce-paper-number: "WP-2026-01"
ce-version: "0.1-draft"
ce-doi: "TBD on first release"
bibliography: references.bib
csl: https://www.zotero.org/styles/american-economic-association
format:
  pdf: default
  html: default
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# Introduction

Curaçao opens its 2026 World Cup against Germany on 14 June. On the FIFA ranking it
sits at 82. With a population of roughly 150,000 it is the smallest country by population
ever to qualify for a World Cup, by a wide margin. The combination, low rank and small
population, looks like an anomaly that needs explaining.

This paper argues that the anomaly is largely an artefact of the measurement system. The
FIFA ranking is an Elo-style metric that rewards frequency of matches against high-rated
opponents. A country that plays a thin qualifying schedule against regional opponents,
even with strong squad-level talent, accumulates ranking points slowly. For small states
with limited domestic competition and short qualifying windows, that is the standing
condition. For small states whose available playing pool sits largely in foreign league
systems through diaspora migration, the ranking misses the part of the squad that is
actually developing as professionals. The talent is there. The system that converts
talent into a ranking number does not see most of it.

The empirical question is how large that measurement gap is. The contribution of this
paper is to put a number on it and to identify the structural conditions under which
the gap becomes large enough to mislead. Section 2 sets out the measurement mechanics.
Section 3 introduces the data and the diaspora-pool variable. Section 4 estimates the
gap for a panel of small CONCACAF and UEFA states. Section 5 returns to the Curaçao
case and discusses what the result implies for how we measure the performance of small
states more broadly.

# How the FIFA ranking is built

[To draft. Cover the 2018 SUM-of-the-pre-match-expectation method, weighting by match
importance and confederation strength, and the implications of low match volume for
small confederation members. Cite FIFA's published methodology and the Elo critique.]

# A measurement gap for small states with diaspora pools

[To draft. Define the diaspora-to-resident ratio. Define eligible-pool depth via FIFA
eligibility rules (one senior cap, parent or grandparent qualification, residency).
Argue that for high-ratio states the ranking input set is restricted to a non-
representative sub-sample of available quality.]

# Data and method

[To draft. Sources: FIFA ranking history (monthly), Transfermarkt squad valuations,
ABC squads dataset (`data/abc_squads_iter1.csv`), national census diaspora figures.
Specification: ranking residual against squad-level Transfermarkt valuation, with
diaspora ratio as moderator.]

```{r}
#| label: fig-rank-vs-value
#| fig-cap: "Placeholder: FIFA ranking versus aggregate Transfermarkt squad valuation, small CONCACAF and UEFA states. Each point is one country."
# Iteration: 1
# library(ggplot2)
# library(dplyr)
# Plot to be added once data pull is complete.
```

# Findings

[To draft. State the headline gap. Show the conditional gap on diaspora ratio. Identify
Curaçao's location in the distribution. Compare against Cabo Verde, Albania, Suriname.]

# Discussion

[To draft. What the result implies for ranking-based decisions, including World Cup
seeding and confederation development funding. Connect briefly to the small-state
measurement literature without overreach.]

# Limitations

[To draft. Squad valuation as proxy for realised quality. Selection on observed
qualification. Diaspora ratio as a coarse instrument for what is actually an
eligibility-pool variable. The Curaçao case is partly a federation-governance story
that this paper does not attempt to identify.]

# Conclusion

The Curaçao result is not an outlier against the FIFA ranking. The ranking is an outlier
against the realised quality available to Curaçao through its diaspora. The same
mechanism predicts further mis-rankings in the small-state-with-diaspora category. For
ranking-based decisions about small states this matters. For development-economics
research that uses national-team performance as a proxy for institutional or human-
capital outcomes it matters more, because the proxy is contaminated by the same
measurement gap.

# References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::
